questions.text: According to the lecture comparison between TCP and UDP, which protocol preserves message boundaries and which protocol guarantees in-order data delivery?
answers: UDP preserves message boundaries, while TCP guarantees in-order data delivery.
TCP preserves message boundaries, while UDP guarantees in-order data delivery.
Both protocols preserve message boundaries and guarantee in-order data delivery.
Neither protocol preserves message boundaries or guarantees in-order data delivery.
questions.explanation: Slide 9 shows that TCP has no message boundaries (represented by ✗) but guarantees in-order data delivery (✓), whereas UDP preserves message boundaries (✓) but does not guarantee in-order data delivery (✗).

questions.text: In the '1 process per client' concurrent server model, why is it critical for the parent process to close the socket file descriptor b (the accepted socket channel)?
answers: To terminate the child process immediately.
To allow the parent to reuse the same socket descriptor for the next listening phase.
To avoid running out of file descriptors and to enable the destruction of the channel once the child and client close their respective descriptors.
To prevent the client from sending additional data to the server.
questions.explanation: Slide 13 explains that closing file descriptor b in the parent is necessary because the parent doesn't need it, there is a risk of running out of file descriptors otherwise, and it enables the destruction of the channel once both the child and client close their ends.

questions.text: What happens by default if a process attempts to write data through a socket that has already been closed by the remote peer?
answers: The system silently discards the write attempt and returns 0 bytes written.
A fatal SIGPIPE signal is received by the writing process.
The write operation blocks indefinitely until the remote peer reconnects.
The system automatically attempts to re-establish the connection using a new port.
questions.explanation: Slide 30 states that if a process attempts to write through a socket that has been closed by the other peer, a SIGPIPE signal is received, which is by default fatal unless a signal handler is installed to override it.

questions.text: How does the address_len parameter behave in the accept(int socket, struct sockaddr *address, socklen_t *address_len) system call?
answers: It is an input-only parameter representing the maximum number of client connections allowed.
It is an output-only parameter that specifies the network port number of the incoming client.
It is a value-result parameter that must initially point to the size of the address structure, and upon return, contains the actual size of the peer address.
It is an optional integer that dictates the connection timeout duration in milliseconds.
questions.explanation: Slide 27 specifies that address_len is a value-result parameter which must be set to the amount of space pointed to by the address parameter before the call.

questions.text: What is the main purpose of calling setsockopt() to enable the SO_REUSEADDR option before calling bind()?
answers: To allow multiple server processes to listen on the exact same port concurrently for load balancing.
To override the behavior where restarting a recently quit server fails with a 'Bind: Address Already in Use' error due to the port remaining in TIME_WAIT.
To allow a server to bypass the socket-creation phase and directly bind a file descriptor.
To switch the socket's communication domain dynamically from Internet to Unix domain.
questions.explanation: Slide 30 notes that when a server quits, the listening port remains busy in the TIME_WAIT state. Restarting the server fails in bind with 'Bind: Address Already in Use'. Using setsockopt() to enable SO_REUSEADDR overrides this.

questions.text: If the 16-bit hex value 0xD04C is stored on an Intel-based (Little-Endian) machine and passed through htons(), what are the byte sequences before and after conversion, respectively?
answers: Before: d0 4c, After: d0 4c
Before: 4c d0, After: 4c d0
Before: 4c d0, After: d0 4c
Before: d0 4c, After: 4c d0
questions.explanation: Slide 16 shows that on an Intel (Little-Endian) machine, the host byte representation of 0xD04C is '4c d0' (low byte first). After htons(), it is converted to Big-Endian/Network Byte Order, resulting in 'd0 4c'.

questions.text: Which functions are suggested by the POSIX.1-2001 standard as modern, protocol-independent replacements for the legacy gethostbyname() and gethostbyaddr() functions?
answers: resolver_name() and resolver_addr()
getaddrinfo() and getnameinfo()
inet_aton() and inet_ntoa()
socket_resolve() and socket_reverse()
questions.explanation: Slide 21 states that POSIX.1-2001 suggests instead the use of getnameinfo() and getaddrinfo() respectively.

questions.text: What is the effect of configuring a server socket's address to INADDR_ANY before binding?
answers: The socket will only accept connections from local loopback interfaces (e.g., 127.0.0.1).
The socket is assigned a special address (0.0.0.0) and will receive incoming connections destined to any of the host machine's IP addresses.
The socket will dynamically change its IP address every time a new client connects.
The socket will reject all external internet traffic, functioning only as a Unix domain socket.
questions.explanation: Slide 26 explains that INADDR_ANY is a special address (0.0.0.0) meaning 'any address', allowing the socket to receive connections from all addresses of the host machine.

questions.text: What is a major disadvantage of using a multithreaded server model over a multi-process (forked child) server model?
answers: It is much more inefficient because threads consume significantly more system memory than processes.
Threads cannot utilize the TCP protocol and are restricted exclusively to UDP communication.
It requires synchronization (locks/mutexes) to manage concurrent access to shared process resources safely.
A thread cannot accept connections from more than one client at a time.
questions.explanation: Slide 14 highlights that while multiple threads handle multiple clients concurrently, the drawback is that it requires synchronization for access to shared resources.

questions.text: If a socket is of type SOCK_DGRAM (UDP), what is the function of calling the connect() system call?
answers: It establishes a 3-way handshake with the server to guarantee a reliable channel.
It specifies a peer address with which the socket is to be associated, restricting datagrams to be sent and received only to and from this peer.
It automatically changes the UDP socket to a TCP stream socket.
It registers the UDP socket with the local DNS daemon to resolve IP addresses.
questions.explanation: Slide 28 explains that if a socket is of type SOCK_DGRAM, the address specifies the peer with which the socket is to be associated (datagrams are sent/received only to/from this peer).

questions.text: In the '1 process per client' model, what must the child process do immediately after being successfully forked?
answers: Call bind() to associate itself with a new port, then call accept().
Close the listening socket a, serve client requests through the connected channel b, close private channel b, and then exit.
Fork another child to handle connection errors, then call connect().
Send a SIGPIPE signal to the parent to notify it of a successful connection hand-off.
questions.explanation: Slide 12 outlines the workflow of the Child process: Close listening socket a, Serve client requests through b, Close private channel b, Exit.

questions.text: Based on the standard TCP connection establishment flow, which function does the server invoke that blocks execution until a client initiates a connection?
answers: listen()
bind()
accept()
connect()
questions.explanation: Slide 22 and Slide 27 show that accept() blocks until a client connects or gets interrupted by a signal.

questions.text: Which of the following functions is designed to convert a 32-bit unsigned integer (such as an IPv4 address) from Host Byte Order to Network Byte Order?
answers: htons()
htonl()
ntohs()
ntohl()
questions.explanation: Slide 15 lists uint32_t htonl(uint32_t hostlong) as the function to convert host to network byte order for a long (32 bits) integer.

questions.text: How does a Serial Server (TCP) loop operate, and what is its primary drawback?
answers: It operates by handling multiple client requests in a round-robin multithreaded loop; its drawback is high CPU utilization.
It opens multiple UDP communication channels concurrently; its drawback is that messages may arrive out of order.
It uses a single loop to accept and completely process each client's requests sequentially; its drawback is that it serves only one client at a time, forcing others to wait or fail.
It delegates every connection to a child process; its drawback is running out of system memory.
questions.explanation: Slide 10 demonstrates that the Serial Server loops to accept and process one client fully before accepting another, causing the drawback of serving only one client at a time, forcing others to wait or fail.

questions.text: In the provided TCP server code example, why does the server program register a signal handler for SIGCHLD that repeatedly calls waitpid(-1, NULL, WNOHANG)?
answers: To notify child processes when a new client tries to connect.
To prevent child processes from becoming zombies by asynchronously reaping terminated child processes without blocking the parent's execution loop.
To terminate the parent server process gracefully when there are no more active clients.
To pause incoming network requests until the child processes have finished processing their current load.
questions.explanation: Slides 32 and 34 show the asynchronous reaping of dead children. Reaping prevents terminated children from consuming system process entries (zombies) without causing the parent process loop to block waiting for them.

questions.text: In the definition of struct sockaddr_in for IPv4 sockets, what is the data type and purpose of the sin_port field?
answers: in_port_t (a 16-bit unsigned integer) representing the port number in network byte order
in_addr_t (a 32-bit unsigned integer) representing the port number in host byte order
sa_family_t (an 8-bit unsigned integer) representing the socket address family
A character pointer pointing to the service name in ASCII format
questions.explanation: Slide 23 shows 'in_port_t sin_port;' and indicates that in_port_t is a uint16_t (defined in <stdint.h>), which is a 16-bit unsigned integer. Network protocols require these fields to be in Network Byte Order.

questions.text: When creating a socket via the socket() system call, what are the standard arguments to create an IPv4 TCP stream socket?
answers: socket(AF_UNIX, SOCK_DGRAM, 0)
socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0)
socket(AF_INET6, SOCK_RAW, 0)
socket(AF_INET, SOCK_DGRAM, IPPROTO_UDP)
questions.explanation: Slide 24 shows the instantiation: if ((sock = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0)) == -1). AF_INET represents the IPv4 domain, and SOCK_STREAM represents the stream (TCP) type.

questions.text: What is the primary difference between inet_aton() and inet_ntoa() based on their usage in the name-resolving examples?
answers: inet_aton() resolves a domain name to an IP, whereas inet_ntoa() resolves an IP back to a domain name.
inet_aton() converts an IPv4 address string (dotted-decimal) into binary form in network byte order, while inet_ntoa() converts a binary IP address in network byte order into a dotted-decimal string.
inet_aton() is only used for IPv6 addresses, whereas inet_ntoa() is restricted to IPv4.
inet_aton() is a system call, whereas inet_ntoa() is a library function compiled only on Solaris.
questions.explanation: Slide 20 shows inet_aton converting an IPv4 dot-number into binary form, and Slide 18 shows inet_ntoa converting a binary network address back into a symbolicip (string format).

questions.text: How does the client-side system call connect() behave differently if the socket is configured as a stream socket (SOCK_STREAM) versus a datagram socket (SOCK_DGRAM)?
answers: For SOCK_STREAM, it sends a message; for SOCK_DGRAM, it closes the socket.
For SOCK_STREAM, it establishes an active network connection with a listening server; for SOCK_DGRAM, it simply associates a default destination peer address for subsequent datagram transmissions.
For SOCK_STREAM, it creates a passive socket; for SOCK_DGRAM, it creates an active socket.
There is no difference; both initiate a 3-way handshake with the server.
questions.explanation: Slide 28 specifies that for TCP stream sockets, a connection is attempted to a listening socket on the server. If the socket is of type SOCK_DGRAM, the address simply specifies the peer with which the socket is to be associated.

questions.text: According to the lecture, how are out-of-order messages arriving at a host machine managed under the Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) before reaching the application?
answers: They are immediately discarded, requiring the sending application to retransmit them.
They are processed in the order they arrive, and the application is responsible for re-sorting them.
They are re-ordered by the receiver's OS network stack, so that the application sees a properly ordered data stream.
TCP does not permit out-of-order packets at the hardware layer; packets are blocked at the router.
questions.explanation: Slide 6 states that under TCP, 'Messages re-ordered by the receiver's OS network stack' and 'Application sees a properly ordered data stream.'
